questions.text: I hold the view that punctuality shows respect for other people's time.
I find that my emotional life is like a well-insulated house — outside conditions don't easily change the internal temperature.
I routinely choose personal development over group activities.
I find that spending my birthday doing something for others feels more special than celebrating myself.
When I have extra money, my first thought is who else could use it.
I tend to wait and see if the group will name the elephant in the room on its own.
I find that the emotional peaks of my day stay vivid in my memory while the neutral moments fade.
I find that my own ambitions are the primary engine of my daily motivation.
When there is a vacuum of leadership, I fill it without thinking twice.
I assume appointments will happen as planned without confirming them again.
I consistently volunteer my expertise to help others solve their problems, even when I receive nothing in return.
I believe that the moral demand of suffering is not something that can be satisfied by sympathy alone — it requires purposeful engagement.
I notice when a seemingly spontaneous remark was actually calculated.
I find that emotional equilibrium during difficult times is a resource I can draw on consistently without it diminishing over time.
I find that my body is a sensitive barometer for every emotional shift in my environment.
I am known for taking decisive stands in group deliberations.
I find that I often miss when a conversation has an unspoken purpose beyond its surface topic.
I don't track how long things take because every situation is different anyway.
I can tell when someone is genuinely surprised versus performing surprise.
I believe that the ability to evaluate human pain without emotional entanglement is what makes certain professions possible.
I find that I set boundaries naturally around my time, energy, and attention.
I maintain version histories of important documents so I can revert changes if needed.
I feel energized when a group reaches a genuine consensus.
I feel that meaningful things can be accomplished without anyone formally leading.
I set expectations with others about response times so they know when to hear from me.
I find that I do not notice when someone is performing a version of themselves for a new audience.
I find that emotional steadiness during others' pain is a quality I regard as one of my most useful characteristics.
I tend to be unaware of the impression I am making on others in the moment.
I am comfortable interrupting a group discussion to redirect its focus.
I am the kind of person whose voice cracks when I'm talking about something meaningful.
I believe that the moral weight of suffering is the one reality that should make every reasonable person pause and reconsider their priorities.
I name files whatever comes to mind and rely on search tools when I need them.
I feel that the moral pull of suffering is the experience that most clearly proves to me that moral obligations are not chosen but discovered.
I feel that my nervous system processes stimulation at a comfortable, manageable rate.
I hold the view that my low emotional reactivity allows me to see situations clearly.
I notice when someone asks a question they already know the answer to in order to make a point.
